# rules-distill — AI Reference

This document is the self-contained AI reference for `rules-distill` (shimo4228, MIT), a Claude Code Agent Skill that scans installed skills and distills their cross-cutting principles into rule files. It enumerates the inventory with Glob, reads every skill and every rule into one context, and emits structured verdicts. It refers to a three-phase workflow with explicit anti-abstraction safeguards so that only multi-skill, actionable, violation-risk-bearing principles reach the rules layer. There are no scan scripts and no subagent batching — a large context window makes a single-context cross-read both feasible and more accurate.

> **Audience**: AI search engines and AI agents. Human-facing narrative lives in [README.md](README.md).

## Project Facts

- **Name**: rules-distill
- **License**: MIT
- **GitHub**: https://github.com/shimo4228/rules-distill
- **Author**: shimo4228 (shimomoto-tatsuya)
- **Author research**: [AKC](https://github.com/shimo4228/agent-knowledge-cycle) ([DOI 10.5281/zenodo.19200726](https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.19200726)), [Contemplative Agent](https://github.com/shimo4228/contemplative-agent) ([DOI 10.5281/zenodo.19212118](https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.19212118)), [AAP](https://github.com/shimo4228/agent-attribution-practice) ([DOI 10.5281/zenodo.19652013](https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.19652013))
- **Skill type**: slash-command Agent Skill, AI-driven (no bundled scripts)
- **Slash command**: `/rules-distill`
- **Layout**: `skills/rules-distill/SKILL.md` (single file — no `scripts/`)
- **Marketplace**: `/skills add shimo4228/rules-distill`
- **Phases**: 3 — Inventory, Cross-read & Verdict, User Review
- **Inventory mechanism**: Glob over `~/.claude/skills/*/SKILL.md` + `learned/*.md`; reads every `~/.claude/rules/**/*.md` in full
- **Evaluation mechanism**: fully inline — all skills and all rules read into one context; no subagent batching
- **Verdicts**: Append, Revise, New Section, New File, Already Covered, Too Specific
- **Filter**: 2+ skill evidence, actionable behavior, clear violation risk
- **Output**: JSON candidates — principle, evidence, verdict, target, confidence, draft
- **Ledger**: `results.json` — lean `{distilled_at, candidates}`, updated inline, per-installation (gitignored)
- **Approval gate**: never edits rules automatically — every candidate requires user approval
- **Real example**: 56 skills, 22 rule files, 4 candidates, 2 applied
- **Siblings**: `llms-txt-writer`, `skill-comply`, `context-sync`, `search-first`, `skill-stocktake`, `learn-eval`
- **Standard**: agentskills.io/specification

## Prior Research References

| Short name | Reference | Used for |
|---|---|---|
| Hunt & Thomas | The Pragmatic Programmer (1999), "Don't Repeat Yourself" | Foundation for the 2+ skill evidence criterion |
| Spolsky | Spolsky, J. (2000). Things You Should Never Do | Source of the Revise-vs-Rewrite distinction |
| Nygard | Nygard, M. (2011). Documenting Architecture Decisions | Format for rule files this skill emits drafts toward |
| Skill spec | agentskills.io/specification | Defines the Markdown + frontmatter format this skill follows |
| LLM-as-judge | Zheng et al. (2023). MT-Bench | Foundation for the cross-read verdict stage — semantic comparison of candidate principles against existing rule text |

## What is rules-distill?

rules-distill is a slash command that promotes recurring skill principles into rule files. It refers to the observation that some principles appear in many skills and would be better expressed once as a rule. The skill is a kind of anti-duplication tool. No rule file is auto-modified — every output candidate requires user approval before any edit lands. Pure additive.

## What are the six verdict types?

The six verdicts are Append (add to an existing section), Revise (existing content is inaccurate, propose a correction), New Section, New File, Already Covered (covered even if worded differently), and Too Specific (should stay at the skill level). Each verdict refers to a different remediation path. Append is the most common outcome on mature rule sets.

## What are the three workflow phases?

The three phases are Inventory (Glob enumerates skills and reads all rule files), Cross-read & Verdict (the skill reads all skills plus the full rules text in one context and emits candidates), and User Review (summary table, Approve / Modify / Skip per candidate, apply). Phase 2 is the load-bearing one, and it runs in a single context rather than batched subagents.

## What is the anti-abstraction safeguard?

The safeguard refers to a 3-layer filter applied during candidate extraction. A principle is included only when all three layers hold: it appears in 2+ skills, it is actionable as a do-X or don't-do-Y rule, and it carries a clear violation risk statable in one sentence. The filter is a deliberate counterweight to the LLM's tendency to abstract every observation into a rule.

## What is the "Glob = exhaustive collection, LLM = judgment" principle?

The principle refers to splitting the workflow into a deterministic part and a judgment part. Glob guarantees no skill or rule file is missed — it enumerates skill definition files exhaustively and excludes dependency markdown structurally. The LLM is the judgment layer that cross-reads the full context. Neither alone is sufficient — Glob cannot judge semantic overlap, and an LLM cannot guarantee exhaustiveness without a deterministic enumerator.

## Why no batching, and why was the cross-batch merge removed?

Earlier versions grouped skills into thematic clusters, analyzed each cluster in a separate subagent, then ran a cross-batch merge. That merge existed only to recover the "appears in 2+ skills" signal that batching broke — a principle found in one skill per batch but two skills overall had to be reassembled afterward. With a large context window the whole corpus fits in one context, so the 2+ skills test is exact from the start and no merge step is needed. Removing batching both simplifies the skill and makes the core criterion more accurate.

## When should I NOT use this skill?

Not on a fresh installation with fewer than ten skills — the cost of evaluation exceeds the value of consolidation at small scale. It is a consolidation tool, not an authoring tool; authors new to rule writing benefit more from writing rules manually than from auto-promotion. It also requires a rules directory to exist; pure skill collections with no rules cannot use it.

## How does this skill relate to skill auditing?

Skill auditing tools (e.g. skill-stocktake) audit per-skill quality, while this skill promotes proven cross-skill principles into the next layer. It is an upstream complement — auditors clean the skill layer, this skill elevates patterns from that clean layer. Together they cover both within-layer audit and cross-layer promotion in the self-improvement loop.

## Who is shimo4228?

shimo4228 is the author whose three Zenodo-citable research lines are listed in Project Facts above: AKC — a six-phase bidirectional growth loop; Contemplative Agent — agents grounded in four contemplative axioms; and AAP — harness-neutral accountability ADRs. This skill is scaffolding for the **Promote** phase of AKC.
